data.title: ZoomInfo Technologies: Growth Acceleration Outlook Intact
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on ZoomInfo Technologies (NASDAQ:GTM), formerly 'ZI' (published just 1 month ago) was an upgrade to a buy rating, as I believed the fundamentals had improved by a lot and the valuation was attractive at 3.4x forward revenue. Indeed, the market view appears to be aligned with mine. The share price has rallied hard from ~$7.8 to $10.31 today. This recent result reinforces my positive outlook. Success in the upmarket shift, the launch of the GTM Studio, and strong Copilot adoption are solid developments that should continue to support growth acceleration.

1Q25 results update

ZI 1Q25 results showed early signs that its change in strategy is working. Total revenue was $305.7 million. While it was down ~1.4% y/y, it is still ahead of the high end of guidance—something I take as a sign of solid execution, especially during this transition phase. This -1.4% y/y revenue growth was also the 3rd straight quarter of y/y growth improvement. Net revenue retention [NRR] also showed positive development as it went from a y/y downtrend to being held steady at 87% for the second straight quarter. Given that the new growth strategy is to retain and expand large enterprise customers, this positive shift in trend suggests that the new strategy is also helping in improving NRR. Lastly, with $124.5 million in unlevered free cash flow, GTM still has plenty of room to invest.

Strategic Pivot to Enterprise Gaining Traction

If you recall my previous write-up last month, a key reason I believed GTM’s growth can continue to improve, is because of its upmarket transition. 1Q25 showed that GTM continues to make progress in this aspect. Management noted that revenue from customers with >100 employees accelerated to 3% y/y in 1Q25. This 3% y/y growth may not be huge on a standalone basis, but given that this cohort now makes up 71% of total annual contract value [ACV]. What this suggests is that the quality of the revenue base is improving. As I’ve pointed out before, the economics in this segment are meaningfully better—larger customers tend to be stickier, spend more, and are generally more predictable.

Customer metrics also continue to point in the right direction too. GTM ended 1Q25 with 1,868 customers with >$100k in ACV, marking the fourth consecutive quarter of sequential improvement, and marked the first quarter of meaningful y/y growth (6.1%). The more important takeaway is that the current situation is in the “stable” phase vs. the “deteriorating” phase in FY24. More impressively, the million-dollar ACV cohort is also growing—both in customer count and in average ACV—which suggests that GTM is landing and expanding well within its largest accounts. Some prominent customer names called out by management include Stripe and Intuit, and they do instill confidence in me that GTM’s solution is working. If this momentum holds and the mix shift plays out as I modelled previously (re-pasted below), I stand firm in my view that revenue growth will accelerate.

GTM Platform Evolution and New Growth Levers

Looking ahead, I think the launch of GTM Studio could be an inflection point for GTM. The ticker change to “GTM” today (13th May) might seem like a simple rebrand at first glance, but in my view, it shows that management is serious about pivoting GTM from just a data vendor to a system of record for how clients run their go-to-market motion. The high-level idea here is that GTM Studio solves a pain point that nearly every enterprise deals with—customer data trapped in silos. By bringing this data together and layering in AI, clients can run their marketing campaigns in a more efficient (cost- and productivity-wise) manner.

We can get a good degree of visibility into the demand for GTM Studio by looking at the demand traction that the core infrastructure (that supports GTM Studio) is already gaining. The operations business (as I said before, this business connects all the data layers today) is now one of GTM’s fastest-growing areas, delivering double-digit growth. Within that, data-as-a-service [DaaS] continues to scale nicely, with new logo wins up 24% y/y and average ACV per customer rising ~10% y/y. That combination of growing adoption and deeper spending is a strong signal that the solution is resonating well with customers.

Copilot is also ramping up nicely, building nicely on the momentum I highlighted previously (crossed $150 million in ACV and was landing with >90% of new logos). Management has also sized up the potential TAM expansion by targeting account managers and executives—a triple in TAM per account—which is massive. The financial impact is becoming tangible. On the 1Q25 call, management confirmed that Copilot is not only bringing in new customers but also driving a clear per-seat pricing uplift as existing users migrate. This is a strong validation that customers see the ROI and are willing to pay more for solutions that enhance their productivity and are also willing to embed more deeply into their GTM workflows.

Valuation

Although the share price has rallied a lot since my last write-up, I think the upside is still very attractive. My growth assumptions stay the same, as the 1Q25 results validated that my view on the business is correct, that growth should accelerate as the mix shifts to larger accounts. In fact, I think my growth assumptions may be too conservative considering that 1Q25 revenue growth is -1.4%, which is already above my -2% estimates for FY25. The market has also shown that it is willing to re-rate GTM’s multiple higher when it shows potential for growth to accelerate (multiples went from low 3 to 3.85x today). As GTM shows more growth acceleration, multiples should move higher.

Risk

The key risk, like I have said, is the pullback from the SMB segment. Even if the current strategy is working well, this drag may keep overall revenue growth looking muted for longer than investors would like. Additionally, GTM Studio needs to continue gaining adoption to support the growth story. On top of that, macro uncertainty is still present. If IT budgets or hiring slow further, it could put more pressure on growth.

Conclusion

My view for GTM is a buy rating. 1Q25 results confirm the upmarket strategy is gaining traction. The launch of the GTM Studio and Copilot engagement are also very positive developments. While near-term headline growth is still impacted by the pullback in the SMB segment, I am still confident that growth can accelerate in the coming years.

data.title: AI Diffusion Rule Rescinded: Tech Giants Impacted
data.description:
Nvidia and The Art of The Deal

After months of lobbying on Capitol Hill, NVIDIA (NVDA) and Republican lawmakers’ efforts are paying off as the Trump administration begins to chip away at Biden-era restrictions on semiconductors.

As of Wednesday, the Trump administration sought to rework legislation enacted by the Biden administration to curb exports of artificial intelligence chips from the United States. Where both presidents have targeted China and other parts of the world with tougher semiconductor restrictions, as the demand grows, access to US chips from various tiers may require greater controls. Shares in NVDA, AMD (AMD), Broadcom (AVGO), and Intel (INTC) surged following the news.

The rule—known as the Framework for Artificial Intelligence Diffusion—would have forced Chinese companies such as Huawei to begin developing their own AI hardware, critics argued, threatening U.S. dominance over the chipmaking industry and hampering AI innovation in key markets. NVDA had joined a number of Republican lawmakers and tech giants, including Microsoft (MSFT), Oracle (ORCL), and Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), in speaking out against the rule.

“We welcome the administration’s leadership and new direction on AI policy,” said an Nvidia spokesperson. “With the AI Diffusion Rule revoked, America will have a once-in-a-generation opportunity to lead the next industrial revolution and create high-paying US jobs, build new US-supplied infrastructure and alleviate the trade deficit.”

The Trump administration recently prioritized negotiations with Middle Eastern countries such as Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates while still cracking down on China’s access to U.S. chips.

"[AI Diffusion] is not a risk with a friend like Saudi Arabia at all but I think in general there was a great deal of misunderstanding about the diversion of GPUs," the White House's AI and crypto czar David Sacks told the Saudi-U.S. Investment Forum in Riyadh on Tuesday, kicking off a wider tour of the region by President Donald Trump.

Humain Deals Reflect Deepening U.S.-Saudi Tech Ties

The AI Diffusion victory followed a number of major wins for NVDA and other tech giants as a result of deepening tech ties between the U.S. and the Middle East. On Tuesday, the company revealed a partnership with Saudi Arabian state-owned AI company Humain to supply semiconductors for up to a 500 megawatt data center buildout, helping transform the country into a “global powerhouse in AI.” That same day, Amazon (AMZN)’s Amazon Web Services unit announced a separate deal with Humain invest more than $5B in the so-called “AI Zone” in the country.

Separately, a Bloomberg report revealed on Tuesday that the Trump administration may strike a deal with the UAE that would allow it to purchase "more than a million" NVDA chips, higher than the current restrictions set in place by Biden officials. Shares in NVDA surged more than 6% on Tuesday following both developments, shooting the semiconductor giant’s market capitalization back above $3T for the first time since February. NVDA extended its gains on Wednesday, climbing 3.6% in afternoon trading.

Big questions loom about how the U.S. will approach chip exports in the long term. Republicans and Democrats are widely aligned on the need to stamp out China’s semiconductor ambitions from a national security standpoint, but finding a path forward that doesn’t weigh on U.S. business will pose a major challenge. However, the uncertainty has done little to weigh on performance at the world’s top chipmakers amid explosive AI demand.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing's (TSM) revenue soared 48.1% year-over-year in April amid increasing demand for its AI chips as electronics firms scrambled to buy key parts before global tariffs came into effect. Shares in the Taiwanese company, which is subject to U.S. export rules given that much of its products rely on American technology, have risen more than 23% over the past month as a result of this performance and loosening trade restrictions around semiconductors.

Meanwhile, a temporary truce between Washington and Beijing, in which the U.S. will reduce its tariffs on China from 145% to 30% and China will reduce its levies from 125% to 10%, which has helped boost investor sentiment for NVDA, TSM and their chipmaking rivals and assuage some fears over how tariffs will impact future earnings.

While the truce is only set to last 90 days, Trump lauded the agreement as a "total reset with China," lifting investor hopes that the worst could be over.

Top Tech Stocks: Which Are The Better Buys?

Semiconductor and AI-leveraging companies are receiving positive tailwinds as the Trump administration continues its efforts to undo Biden-era chip restrictions and the U.S.-China trade war begins to cool. Tech giants including (NVDA) (+20.2%), (TSM) (+25%), (AMD) (+24.7%), (AMZN) (+15.5%), (MSFT) (+15.8%), and (ORCL) (+20.7%) have all climbed higher over the past month, and I expect this momentum to continue. The question is, which ones are worth your investment?

Seeking Alpha Quant’s Top Technology Stocks and Top Semiconductor Stocks screeners can help you identify Strong Buy-rated stocks with excellent fundamentals. Alternatively, you can also use SA’s comparison tool to evaluate two stocks side-by-side. In this article, I’ll be using the tool to compare two of the most sought-after semiconductor stocks, NVDA and TSM, to determine which is the better buy based on Quant Factor Grades.

NVDA’s 1Y Price Return Outpaces TSM and S&P 500

Over the past year, NVDA has outperformed both TSM and the broader market. However, NVDA is currently rated as a Hold by SA’s Quant System, while TSM remains a Strong Buy, suggesting that TSM’s Quant fundamentals could be more highly rated.

Ratings and Quant Rankings: NVDA vs. TSM

Let's examine the quantitative figures more closely to determine which is the better buy. Let the battle of the chipmakers begin.

Nvidia (NASDAQ: NVDA)

  • Market Capitalization: $3.17T

  • Quant Rating: Hold

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 5/14/25): 108 out of 540

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 5/14/25): 13 out of 68

After being down nearly 21% from its January 6 peak of $149.42 amid growing competition from China's DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) and a contentious trade war, NVDA’s recent deals in the Middle East and cooling trade tensions between the U.S. and China have helped it bounce back significantly. Now 12% away from its peak, NVDA’s upcoming Q1 earnings may suffer less of a hit than previously thought. In a bull case for NVDA, SA analyst Cash Flow Venue writes:

The recent "truce in Geneva" between the U.S. and China, reducing tariffs, has positively impacted Nvidia and the broader market. Nvidia is well-positioned to navigate any future trade turmoil or escalations, maintaining its robust growth trajectory.

While I do agree with their assessment, NVDA has remained a Hold in SA’s Quant System in recent years, aside from a few brief periods in 2024 and 2021. Much of this has been due to its expensive ‘F’-rated valuation grade, which I’ll discuss in further detail below.

NVDA 3Y Quant Summary

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (NYSE: TSM)

  • Market Capitalization: $824.8B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 3/14/25): 23 out of 540

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 3/14/25): 2 out of 68

I previously cited Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) as one of the stocks that could be most impacted by Trump’s tariffs on China and escalating trade tensions between Washington and Beijing. Those challenges paled compared to the Taiwanese chipmaker’s fantastic growth outlook and solid valuation, and look even less problematic now that the U.S. and China have reached a temporary truce.

One of the world’s largest companies by market cap and ranking among the most valuable, TSM is riding high from strong quarterly results, with Q1 FY2025 revenue of ~$25.53 billion (+35.3% Y/Y) and diluted EPS of ~$2.12 (+60% Y/Y). The company recently broke ground on its third plant in Phoenix, Arizona with the goal to manufacture more than 30% of its most advanced chips in the U.S. The latest TSMC Phoenix site will help the company further shield its operations from political uncertainty in Taiwan and support bipartisan efforts in the U.S. to increase domestic chip production.

As SA analyst Oakoff Investments puts it, “TSMC remains a top AI investment due to its dominant market position, strategic alliances, and cutting-edge technologies, despite geopolitical risks.”

TSM 1Y Quant Summary

TSM is currently rated a Strong Buy by SA’s Quant system. Let’s take a deeper look at its fundamentals compared to NVDA using SA Quant’s comparison tool:

Valuation: NVDA vs. TSM Stock

That doesn’t mean it’s all bad. As I wrote in my recent piece, Nvidia Stock Is Down 21% From Its Peak. Is It Time To Buy?, NVDA’s forward PEG ratio of 0.79 comes at a 52% discount to the wider technology sector median, reflecting a decent price for NVDA’s strong growth outlook. However, lower-rated metrics, including a price-to-sales ratio of 14.79, a 417% difference vs. the sector median, suggest that other elements of the stock are overvalued.

TSM, on the other hand, offers a solid ‘C’ valuation grade with a forward Non-GAAP PEG of 0.95, nearly a 44% discount from the sector median. Additionally, the company possesses a forward EV/EBITDA that is more than a 30% discount from the sector.

Momentum: NVDA vs. TSM Stock

Both companies boast solid momentum amid surging demand for AI chips. NVDA’s ‘B-’ momentum grade is upheld by a strong one-year price performance of 44.5%, a 4,000%+ difference from the sector. TSM’s momentum grade is similarly rated a ‘B-,’ with one-year price performance of 25.3%, 6,433.2%.

Growth: NVDA vs. TSM Stock

SA’s Quant System awards NVDA an ‘A’ in terms of growth. Forward revenue growth of 59.7% remains strong, a 775% difference from the wider technology sector's 6.8%.

TSM earns a ‘B+’ in terms of growth, with lower forward revenue growth of 27.5%. Forward EBITDA growth of 27.8% also fails to match up to NVDA’s 66.7% in the category. TSM faces a number of challenges when it comes to growth, including capacity constraints—with demand outpacing supplies—and has launched three new manufacturing plants in Arizona among other planned expansions as a result.

Profitability: NVDA vs. TSM Stock

Both NVDA and TSM shine in terms of profitability, scoring overall A+’s in the category. While both are compared to different sectors by our Quant system. Both score sector-leading marks in metrics such as cash from operations ($64.1B and $60.6, respectively), return on total capital, and return on total assets.

Conclusion: NVDA Excels In Growth, But TSM Is Less Risky And More Affordable

While NVDA’s growth is slightly stronger, its temporary dip in share price is swiftly reversing as trade relations improve between the U.S. and China. With a cheaper valuation and strong Quant attributes across the board, TSM is the safer and more affordable bet. You can compare all the metrics side-by-side using this comparison page generated by Seeking Alpha Quant.

The stocks featured here can help complement a diverse portfolio. Alternatively, consider exploring Alpha Picks if you're seeking a limited number of Seeking Alpha Quant’s best monthly ideas from the hundreds of top quant Strong Buy-rated stocks.

data.title: TORM: Some Positive Developments And Cheap Valuation
data.description:
Introduction

I last wrote about TORM plc (NASDAQ:TRMD) in early April when the shipping stocks were facing quite a lot of headwinds due to concerns regarding trade tensions. The stock of various shipping companies, including TRMD, tanked partly in anticipation of these trade tensions and partly due to normalizing Time Charter Equivalent (TCE) rates.

However, the TCE rates are now stabilizing, and the trade tension also seems to be cooling down. Moreover, the current worldwide fleet consists of a lot of old vessels that are poised to retire in the near to medium term. Given these improving dynamics and a lucrative valuation, I have upgraded the stock from soft buy to buy.

Some positive developments

Earlier last week, TRMD came out with a decent set of numbers, with its top line beating the consensus estimates but its bottom-line being in line with the estimates. The company reported a 26% decline in topline to $330 million, beating the consensus estimates by $88 million, while GAAP EPS came in at $0.64, which was in line with the consensus estimates.

While the headline numbers are not so exciting, the underlying fundamentals are developing in a positive way for TRMD. In my last article, I had mentioned the declining TCE rates that TRMD and the shipping industry as a whole were struggling with. The TCE rates fell dramatically in 2024. However, the continuous fall in TCE rate has stabilized in the 1Q25 quarter. While the TCE rates are down ~38% YoY to ~$27K per day, the rates are in line with what they were in 4Q24, indicating a stabilization in the rates. Specifically, the LR2s were ~$34K per day and LR1s were ~$25K per day, which are consistent with the levels seen during 4Q24.

On the demand side, the product tanker industry has not much benefited from the Red Sea disruptions of late. Although the ships have to take longer detours from the Cape of Good Hope instead of going through the Red Sea to facilitate trade flow toward Europe, increasing ton-miles. The overall reduction in trade volume from the Middle East to Europe has negated much of the benefit from higher ton-miles. The trade between the Middle East to Europe has dropped ~40%, leading to fewer shipments. While recently a ceasefire agreement has been reached by the Houthis and the US, it is still disputed as to if this ceasefire can hold for long. Therefore, I still believe this disruption to be in place for quite some time.

While the Red Sea issue continues to be a neutral event of TRMD, a more eventful trigger is taking place in Europe. The European diesel demand has been rising of late, primarily due to increased demand for marine gas oil due to the Mediterranean emission control area starting from May 2025. This increased demand, when combined with the closing of three refineries in Northwest Europe, could result in higher imports of diesel into Europe from the Middle East. As per TORM’s management, an additional ~140K barrels a day of combined diesel and jet fuel supply would need to be imported by the year's end into Europe, translating into additional demand of 12-19 LR2 equivalents per year.

Moreover, on the demand front, the recent trade tension episode has finally cooled down, with the US and China finally lowering tariff rates for each other for the next 90 days. I believe this is quite a good development for the shipping companies as well as global growth, which was earlier forecasted to be sluggish thanks to these tensions.

With respect to supply-side dynamics, there is a relatively high product tanker order book currently with the shipbuilders. However, the average age of the fleet has also been on the higher side for the last few years. ~15% of the worldwide product tanker fleet is more than 20 years old. This old fleet of product tankers should get retired in the near to medium term, as operating older vessels is more cost-intensive. The vessel retirement should offset a large part of fleet growth. Moreover, ordering of new vessels has come to a standstill in 1Q25, reaching the lowest quarterly levels in the last 3 years for LR1s, LR2s and MRs.

Additionally, a lot of vessels are switching back to dirty trade from clean trade. The LR2 and Aframax markets are integrated, with a lot of vessels swinging between clean and dirty trade based on the return on investment. Earlier, the ROI was much in favor of clean trades. However, this has now normalized with falling TCE rates. Hence, the fleet has declined by ~20 vessels since 4Q24, even though 24 new vessels were introduced in the market. This means more than 40 vessels have left the clean trade market for the dirty trade market. This dynamic of vessel switching toward dirty trade should benefit TRMD, as much of its capacity is utilized in clean trade. Moreover, switching from dirty to clean trade requires a considerable cost as well as time. Hence, the operator would need considerable visibility and returns to switch back to the clean trade market again.

TORM’s valuations are quite lucrative

As for valuation, TRMD is rated a solid A+ as per seeking alpha quant rating. The company is currently trading at a forward non-GAAP PE ratio of ~6.02x, which is quite cheap when compared to the sector median. While there are still some EPS headwinds going forward, I believe the current PE ratio is quite lucrative.

However, to gauge the downside risk for TRMD stock, I would like to use the price-to-book ratio. The company is currently trading at ~0.75x price-to-book ratio (TTM), which signals that the current market cap is implying that the company is valued less than the net assets that the company currently holds in its books. I believe this assumption by the market to be quite pessimistic.

Moreover, if we go by the broker valuation of TRMD’s vessel, the company looks even more undervalued. As per the average broker valuations of TRMD’s fleet, the company has a fleet worth ~$3.1 billion as compared to an enterprise value of ~$2.45 billion.

Risks to consider

Trade tensions may resurface

Although, as per the latest US-China tariff negotiations, the tariffs by the US, as well as China, have been reduced significantly, it is still a temporary deal with an expiration period of 90 days. Moreover, the reduced tariff rates for the rest of the world are also temporary, with a potential for higher tariffs if no trade deal has been negotiated between the US and its counterparts. This puts the risk of tariff concerns resurfacing once this 90-day negotiation window expires.

Conclusion

TRMD has been facing some headwinds like normalizing TCE rates as well as tariff-related uncertainties. However, recently, there has been positive development on both fronts. Moreover, the current worldwide fleet consists of a lot of old vessels that are poised to retire in the near to medium term. Given improving dynamics and lucrative valuations, I upgrade my rating on TRMD from soft buy to a buy.

data.title: Daktronics: Strong Margins, Solid Cash, But Taxes Are A Drag
data.description:
Investment thesis

Daktronics Inc. (DAKT) is a fundamentally sound company that has evolved into a diversified provider of visual display systems. Since 2021, it has delivered stronger performance through pricing gains, larger project wins, and operational improvements. This resulted in higher revenue, better margins, and improved returns on capital.

The company is financially strong, with a low debt-to-equity ratio, low reinvestment needs, and consistent cash generation. However, its historically high effective tax rate remains a drag on net profitability. My valuation, taking account of this high tax rate, did not provide any margin of safety.

Business background

Over the past decade, Daktronics has evolved from a niche scoreboard manufacturer to a global provider of visual communication ecosystems. Today, the company is primarily engaged in the design, manufacture, and support of electronic display systems.

The company reported its performance under 5 business segments as illustrated in the left part of Chart 1, with the Live Events segment being the biggest revenue contributor. As shown in the right part of Chart 1, Daktronics is essentially a US-centric business.

Note that Daktronics has April as its financial year-end. Unless stated otherwise, the years in this article refer to the financial years. The 2025 performance in this article relates to the Jan 2025 LTM performance.

The US digital display market is a single-digit growth one, as exemplified by the following market research reports.

“The US digital signage market is estimated at USD 9.16 billion in 2025 and is expected...at a CAGR of 7.88% during the forecast period (2025 to 2030)” Mordor Intelligence.

“The U.S. digital signage market size was estimated at USD 6,364.1 million in 2024 and is anticipated to grow at a CAGR of 6.6% from 2025 to 2030.” Grand View Research

Based on these reports, I would estimate that Daktronics had about 9% to 13% of the US market.

Structural changes

Looking at Chart 1, and ignoring the Covid-19 year of 2021, you can see 2 distinct revenue periods:

  • 2016 to 2020 with an average annual revenue of USD 590 million.
  • Post-2021 with an average annual revenue of USD 750 million.

The reasons for the better post-2021 performance are:

  • Price increases beginning in FY2022 and continuing into FY2023 and FY2024 to offset rising input costs and maintain profitability.
  • Securing larger projects across its Live Events, Commercial, and International segments.
  • Expansion into high-resolution LED markets.
  • Improved supply chain conditions, especially post-pandemic.

The better revenue performance translated into better returns, with the ROIC averaging 9% post-2021 compared to the average 2% from 2016 to 2020. Its ROE averaged 5% post-2021 compared to the 2% average from 2016 to 2020.

Excluding the supply chain factors, most of the above reasons for better performance seemed to be structural. As such, I am more likely to view the post-2021 revenue level as sustainable, supported by the following underlying trends. Refer to Chart 2.

  • Post-2021, while the gross profit margin declined initially, the gross profit margins over the past 2 years were higher than those pre-2021.
  • The Selling, General and Administration [SGA] margins have also been declining since peaking in 2019.
  • The better gross profits and SGA margins are reflected in the better contribution margin in 2024/25.

Notes to Chart 2:

I broke down the operating profits into fixed costs and variable costs.

  • Fixed cost = SGA, Depreciation & Amortization, R&D and Others.
  • Variable cost = Cost of Sales – Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Contribution = Revenue – Variable Cost.
  • Contribution margin = Contribution/Revenue.

As shown on the left side of Chart 2, fixed costs account for approximately one-quarter of total costs. With this cost structure, increases in revenue do not significantly improve profit unless accompanied by gains in operational efficiency. Therefore, greater emphasis should be placed on improving productivity and cost efficiency to expand margins.

Unfortunately, there were no specific discussions on the “productivity” or “efficiency” efforts in the 2022 to 2024 Forms 10k. Rather, they were implied or contextual, with the most explicit in 2024:

“Fewer supply chain and operational disruptions paired with our investments to increase capacity allowed for improved operational efficiency…”

Nevertheless, the trends seem to be pointing in the correct direction as exemplified by the following:

  • Gross profitability improved from 26% in 2022 to 39% in 2025.
  • Asset turnover improved from 139% in 2022 to 153% in 2025.
  • There was no change in the cash conversion cycle between 2022 and 2025.

Peer comparison

I compared Daktronics' performance with the following listed companies providing similar products and services.

  • Acuity Inc. is a leading industrial technology company specializing in advanced lighting and building management solutions.
  • Barco NV is a Belgian company that develops visualization solutions, including digital signage displays and systems.
  • Leyard Optoelectronic is a Chinese company that specializes in LED display products and solutions, serving various markets globally.
  • LSI Industries is a US company that provides non-residential lighting and retail display solutions, serving the commercial and industrial sectors.

Daktronics is among the smaller companies in terms of 2025 revenue and is ranked in the middle in terms of revenue growth rate. Refer to Table 1.

Growth needs to be funded, and one metric for this is the Reinvestment rate (Reinvestment/NOPAT), where:

Reinvestment = CAPEX & Acquisitions – Depreciation & Amortization + increase in Net Working Capital.

You can see that Daktronics had one of the lowest Reinvestment rates at 8%, signaling good capital efficiency. Note that Barco had an average negative rate because its Depreciation & Amortization more than offset the amount spent on CAPEX & Acquisition.

Notes to Table 1:

  • Acuity has Aug as its financial year-end, and the 2025 results are based on its Feb 2025 LTM results.
  • To “synchronize” the financial years, I took Barco and Leyard's 2015 to 2024 results as the 2016 to 2025 results.
  • LSI has Jun as its financial year-end, and the 2025 results are based on its Mar 2025 LTM results.

I compared Daktronics' performance with that of its peers based on the following metrics. Refer to Charts 3 to 6. The overall picture is that Daktronics is operationally sound, with a balanced but unexceptional financial profile:

  • I would rate Daktronics' gross profitability and ROE performance as average compared to its peers.
  • Daktronics had the lowest gross profit margin compared to its peers. Post-2021, except for Leyard, Daktronics, and the other peers had improving gross profit margins.
  • Daktronics had one of the lowest SGA margins compared to its peers. Furthermore, post-2021, there was no significant deterioration in the SGA margin. Of the peers, 2 companies’ SGA margin got worse in 2025 compared to 2022.
  • Daktronics had the lowest DE ratio compared to its peers for many years, although its DE ratio had risen to be about the average in 2025.
  • Initially, Daktronics ranked in the middle in terms of Total Liabilities/Total Assets, but since 2022, it has had the highest ratio. The discrepancy between the DE ratio and the Total Liabilities/Total Assets ratio implied that while not debt-heavy, it still has substantial obligations, just not ones that accrue interest.

Financial Position

I would rate Daktronics as financially sound based on the following.

As of the end, Jan 2025, it had USD 132 million in cash, equal to about 25% of its total assets. The peers’ cash-to-total assets ratio ranged from 1% to 30%, with an average (excluding Daktronics) of 15%.

It has a DE ratio of 16% as of the end of Jan 2025. This has come down from its 2024 high of 25%. The peers’ DE ratios ranged from 10% to 47%.

From 2016 to 2025, there was only 1 year that Daktronics failed to generate positive cash flow from operations. During this period, it had a good cash flow conversion rate – it generated a total of USD 325 million in cash flow from operations compared to the total PAT of 72 million.

I have already mentioned its low Reinvestment rate that averaged 8% over the past decade. This meant that a big part of the NOPAT could be returned to shareholders.

It had a good capital allocation plan as shown in Table 2. The cash flow from operations was sufficient to fund its CAPEX and Acquisition, with some excess returned to shareholders.

My main concern is the high effective tax rate that averaged 49% (excluding the exceptionally high tax rate of 2022) over the past decade. Even if I considered a longer period, the average effective tax rate from 2003 to 2025 (excluding 2022) was 39%.

Both the historical tax rates were well above the statutory US federal tax rate. This seems to be due to a combination of structural issues (valuation allowances), specific non-deductible items (e.g., fair value adjustments, goodwill), and multi-jurisdictional tax rates.

Valuation

My analysis showed that Daktronics is fundamentally sound from both a track record perspective as well as in comparison with its peers. But it is not a high-growth company as it only achieved a 3.8% CAGR in revenue over the past decade. The sector it serves is also a single-digit growth one.

As such, I adopted the following picture in valuing Daktronics.

  • The base revenue would be the 2025 revenue with a perpetual revenue growth rate of 4%.
  • The contribution margin and capital efficiency would be the 2023 to 2025 average values. These would remain the same over the valuation period.
  • The Reinvestment rate would be the past decade average rate and remain unchanged over the valuation period.
  • The fixed cost would be pegged to the average 2023 to 2025 fixed cost margin.
  • The tax rate would be the 2003 to 2025 average tax rate of 39% (excluding the exceptionally high rate of 2020).

On such a basis, I obtained an intrinsic value of USD 12.96 per share compared to its market price (12 May 2025) of USD 14.37 per share.

Valuation model

I valued Daktronics based on a single-stage Free Cash Flow to the Firm (FCFF) model, as shown in Table 3.

The basic equations used in the model are:

FCFF = EBIT(1 – t) – Reinvestment.

EBIT = Revenue X Contribution margin – Fixed cost

The contribution margin and fixed cost margin are based on the operating model shown in the left part of Chart 2.

The Reinvestment was based on the Reinvestment margin.

The WACC used in the valuation model was based on the first page results of a Google search for “Daktronics WACC”. Refer to Table 4.

Risks and limitations

The main valuation risk is the tax rate. I have assumed the past 2 decades' average tax rate of 39%. If I had assumed a 21% nominal tax rate, the intrinsic value would be USD 16.27 per share. While higher, it still does not provide my target 30% margin of safety.

I am not a tax expert, so I will leave it to you to see whether the company can go against 2 decades of history to deliver a significantly lower tax rate than 39%.

Are there upsides? In my valuation, I have assumed a 27% contribution margin. This is lower than the 2024 and 2025 margins of 29% and 28%. If I assumed the 2024 contribution margin, the intrinsic value increases to USD 17.44 per share.

But until I see an uptrend in the contribution margin (refer to the left part of Chart 2), I think it would be very optimistic to assume a higher contribution margin.

Conclusion

Daktronics is a fundamentally sound company that has successfully evolved beyond its scoreboard roots into a competitive player in the digital display space. However, it operates in a low-growth industry and has only delivered a 3.8% CAGR in revenue over the past decade.

Nevertheless, the company has made structural improvements post-2021.

  • There were higher revenue levels, averaging around USD 750 million annually, well above the USD 590 million pre-2021 average.
  • Gross profit margins, while initially compressed, recovered meaningfully by FY2024.
  • SGA margins trended downward, reflecting consistent cost control.

Operational efficiency also improved, with better asset turnover and a stable cash conversion cycle, despite the increased scale. Daktronics is also financially sound with strong cash flows and low reinvestment needs.

That said, valuation is constrained by two key factors: a historically high effective tax rate and the lack of a clear uptrend in contribution margins. Based on these, my valuation did not provide any margin of safety.

data.title: Palantir: What The Bears Keep Missing
data.description:
Despite Palantir Technologies’ (PLTR) strong run since my early April bullish rating, the bearish sentiment is building up again, fueled by a double top near $125 and renewed concerns over valuation and dilution. This narrative is missing the fundamental inflection point.

Palantir’s $1 billion U.S. commercial business is accelerating 71% YoY, converting pilots to multi-year, enterprise-sized deals in weeks. With 139 $1 million+ contracts on the books and a Rule of 40 score of 83%, the firm is ramping up profits, not just growing. Its asset-light, ontology-driven AI infrastructure produces 42% free cash flow margins without the CapEx tail of peers. Bulls see monetization depth; the bears are looking at multiples.

Palantir is not being priced like a SaaS vendor, they’re pricing the AI operating system for defense, healthcare, and the enterprise. The double top is not a ceiling but a recalibration breather in a longer-term rerating based on execution, embedded systems, and platform stickiness in mission-critical spaces.

Breaking the Bear Case: Why Palantir's Critics Misread the Playbook

Critics see Palantir as stretched both technologically and operationally as well as financially. They cite the "double top" charting around $125, high valuation multiples, stock-comp-based dilution, and lack of growth overseas as warning signs that the stock has moved ahead of its fundamentals.

Some go as far as comparing the scenario with the post-hype downfall of Snowflake (SNOW), surmising that Palantir too could suffer the same fate. These criticisms, though not baseless, rest ultimately on flawed assumptions and too simplistic in premise and prove self-serving and shortsighted. They miss the fundamental transformation of Palantir from a high-beta vision stock into an enterprise-grade infrastructure layer that has built-in AI-powered leverage.

Of course, there was a "double top," but price action in a vacuum means nothing when fundamentals change. Q1 earnings reported not just good numbers but a structural rerating process. Palantir's U.S. commercial business hit a $1 billion+ annual run rate with 19% sequential growth and 71% year-over-year growth. It booked $810 million of contract value in the quarter, 183% year-over-year growth, and turned pilots into multi-year contracts within weeks, not quarters. At a time when sales cycles for enterprise software are lengthening, Palantir is doing the opposite. This is not a speculative high; it's operating velocity finding product-market fit at scale.

Critics cite dilution as an existential threat. Yes, Palantir’s diluted count of shares rose by a little over 155 million in Q1. The market misses the productivity of the dilution. The $19 billion of market value outstanding wasn’t given away for free; it funded a Rule of 40 metric of 83%, 42% operating margins on free cash flow, and enterprise growth that’s compounding into sustainable top-line expansion and long-term value.

Adjusted operating income increased by $391 million on an 82% gross margin basis with only 21% growth in operating expenses compared with 39% growth in revenues. Thus, Palantir is benefiting from dilution at a premium, as opposed to numerous peers with SBCs hiding operating losses.

Additionally, the bear story conveniently overlooks the asset-light business model that Palantir operates. There is no GPU CapEx arms race for the company, no data center bloat, no hyperscaler cost drag. With $5.4 billion of cash and treasuries, zero debt, and minimal CapEx needs, Palantir is in the position to scale through intellectual capital, its ontology layer and AIP platform, free from physical infrastructure. That creates exponential software deployment with no exponential cost, the opposite of Snowflake, which needs perpetual CapEx and data warehousing infrastructural growth. The bears that compare the two overlook the fundamentally different capital structure and margin profiles.

Valuation is a legitimate issue, but the notion that Palantir needs to trade at 70x forward EV/sales in perpetuity to sustain its price misapprehends platform economics. Palantir is not being priced like a SaaS tool, it's being priced like a core AI operating system. AIP agents are displacing incumbent workflow, not supplementing them. Walgreens, AIG, BP, and R1 RCM have all spoken publicly about how Palantir has delivered transformative results, including automating 384 billion store-level decisions or reducing weeks of underwriting to the space of a minute. These aren't incremental applications, they are systemic substitutions for incumbent decision architecture.

And what about international weakness? European commercial revenue fell 5% YoY, but this is a pacing issue, not a demand issue. AI skepticism and European regulatory lag hold up adoption, but they don't negate it. Palantir is favoring the U.S., where ROI is immediate and margin-rich. As governments around the world begin to adopt systems like Maven Smart (now deployed throughout NATO), followed by commercial verticals, this backlog will convert. The international pipeline is not broken, only deferred.

Finally, skeptics are looking for an “Nvidia (NVDA) moment” to justify Palantir. But Palantir is not Nvidia. Its advantage is not model performance, but rather orchestration, which transforms LLMs into agents and then deploys them in production. The moat is not compute, context. That is why Palantir cannot be replaced, and that is why investors are starting to price in.

From Battlefield to Boardroom: Palantir’s $1B Commercial Breakout and the Rise of Enterprise AI

Palantir's evolution from a government contractor to a commercial enterprise software platform is no longer a vision, and now it's quantifiable. The U.S. commercial segment today has a revenue run rate exceeding $1 billion annually, having achieved 71% YoY and 19% quarter-over-quarter in Q1, representing 29% of total revenue, up from 22% last year.

Importantly, the expansion is a function not of pilot contract volumes or license purchases of one or two, but of multi-year, multi-million-dollar enterprise deals. Contract wins, such as the $26 million five-year ACV deal with a prominent healthcare provider and a $19 million deal with an international bank, demonstrate AIP's value across various verticals, not merely in defense-aligned sectors.

Most impressively, Palantir secured 139 deals for $1 million or more, of which 31 involved $10 million or more, highlighting enterprise-wide deployment momentum. These deals are increasingly multi-year, enterprise-scale contracts, such as a five-year $26 million healthcare deal closed mere weeks after an onboarding bootcamp. This pace, it's evidence of “time to value”, is Palantir’s most powerful moat.

This expansion also illustrates enhanced onboarding speed for customers. What used to last a quarter now occurs in weeks. According to management, pilots typically become multi-year commitments within a quarter or two, enabling a more scalable go-to-market engine. Growth in the U.S. commercial total contract value to $810 million (up 183% YoY) confirms this move. Significantly, the Palantir storyline has matured: instead of touting itself as a sovereign arms dealer in software, it now highlights enterprise productivity improvement and AI integration that appeals more to the CFOs than to the generals.

However, Palantir's international commercial segment is a very different case, which reported a decline in revenue of 5% YoY and 11% QoQ. Europe, in particular, remains behind due to the presence of regulatory hurdles as well as AI skepticism. Consequently, the near-term scalability of the company relies significantly on sustained U.S. commercial traction, a geographic risk that cannot be overlooked.

Revenue Quality: Concentration vs. Retention Dynamics

While the customer base grew 39% YoY to 769, Palantir is still vulnerable to revenue concentration. Its top 20 customers spent $70 million each within the trailing twelve months, up 26% YoY, suggesting increasing wallet penetration but also dependence.

Government clients, particularly in the U.S., continue to provide the foundation for Palantir's base as a source of revenue that constitutes 55% of revenue in Q1. Though commercial expansion is strong, the dual dependence by the company on the government budget cycle and a small group of enterprise clients creates the risk of volatility.

However, the quality of these customers is getting better. Net-dollar retention was up from 120% in Q4 to 124% in Q1 on the strength of strong upselling within the AIP. This metric is particularly impressive as it does not include new customers acquired in the last 12 months, which are converted very fast and are not reflected in the retention metric yet.

More importantly, the backlog continues to grow: remaining deal value was up 45% YoY to $5.97 billion, and remaining performance obligations [RPO] climbed to $1.9 billion, a 46% increase. Yet, the gap between international and U.S. momentum is significant. The U.S. contributed 71% of Q1 revenue last year compared to 63% in the prior year, which means Palantir is getting more geographically concentrated. Top-line deceleration may result for the company should U.S. government spending or enterprise AI deployment slow.

Valuation: The Rule of 83 and the Platform Economy

Palantir's current market value of around $300 billion represents a dramatic re-rating by the market, particularly given the company's full-year 2025 revenue outlook of $3.89-$3.90 billion. This puts its forward EV/revenue multiple at about 71x, assuming minimal net debt on the balance sheet in the form of $5.4 billion in cash and treasuries.

This represents a far cry from the ~14x EV/revenue range reported in the early stages of its 2024 reacceleration phase, indicating that Palantir is today valued more similarly to a high-growth infrastructure-grade platform rather than a traditional SaaS company.

This valuation, which appears to be stretched on conventional metrics (e.g., 204x forward P/E, 72x forward price/sales), is arguably a result of Palantir’s believed shift from an “application software” company to a category-defining AI infrastructure player. The market seems to be factoring in:

  • A consistent Rule of 40 of 80% or more puts Palantir in the top tier of software efficiency.

  • Long-term free cash flow expansion, guided to $1.6-$1.8 billion in adjusted FCF for FY25.

  • A platform-based moat through AIP and ontology that allows enterprise AI independence with much reduced switching risk.

From a valuation perspective, this warrants a premium multiple, as investors are willing to pay more for enterprises capable of high-growth rates and financing that growth internally. Most high-growth technology businesses scale on a negative margin or burn capital; Palantir, on the other hand, is compounding free cash flow while speeding up the growth of the top line.

The Rule of 40 also derisks the business model, as it indicates that the business is not predicated on unrestrained expenditure or dilutive in the long run. As long as Palantir can sustain a Rule of 40 score above 60–70%, particularly at its scale, this confirms that Palantir warrants a valuation more commensurate with that of a platform infrastructure leader than a repeat-play SaaS business.

Yet, trading above 71x forward EV/Revenue, there is potential for dramatic multiple compression in the event of a deviation in execution, particularly in international expansion or government renewal phases. There is an implicit assumption in this valuation that Palantir's AI stack will go on to form the standard operating infrastructure in defense, healthcare, financial services, and industrial supply chains worldwide.

Therefore, Palantir isn't valued as a government contractor anymore or as a piece of software. It's being valued as a system AI operating system. Whether the premium holds up remains to be seen and depends on sustained enterprise agent adoption, margin resilience, and category leadership.

Bottom Line

Despite technical pullbacks and valuation angst, Palantir is fundamentally rerating, not peaking. Its $1 billion U.S. commercial engine is scaling rapidly, with an 83% Rule of 40 efficiency, and 42% free cash flow margin. This isn’t hype; it’s infrastructure. The double top isn’t a ceiling, it’s a pause before deeper platform repricing.

data.title: The Wide Range Of Possibilities For Nuclear Energy
data.description:
By Sam Korus

What does the future hold for nuclear and solar energy? Will nuclear struggle to become more relevant with potential capital costs of $26 trillion, or will next-gen reactors make nuclear more competitive than solar power?

Historically, regulatory hurdles have stifled nuclear’s promise of low-cost, reliable baseload power.1 Since 2020, a shift in sentiment has reignited the momentum behind nuclear, but will it ever be competitive with renewables like solar? While regulations have increased the cost of nuclear power dramatically during the past 50 years, the cost of solar power has been declining steadily, as shown below.

Source: ARK Investment Management LLC, 2025, based on data from Lovering et al. 2016; IAEA 2023; EIA 2022; World Nuclear News 2022; and Our World in Data 2023 as of October 2023.2 For informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security. Past performance is not indicative of future results.

Even if nuclear costs had continued to fall in line with the trend in place before regulations derailed them in the 1970s, a quick glance would suggest that they still would not be as low today as solar on a cost-per-watt basis, as shown by the dotted purple trendline above. Importantly, however, the first glance misses a key point: nuclear plants operate at more than 80% utilization, while solar plants operate at a capacity utilization rate in the low 20s%. Adjusted for utilization, the playing field shifts, as shown below.

Source: ARK Investment Management LLC, 2025, based on data from Lovering et al. 2016; IAEA 2023; EIA 2022; World Nuclear News 2022; and Our World in Data 2023 as of October 2023.3 For informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security. Past performance is not indicative of future results.

Using assumptions from Tesla’s (TSLA) Master Plan Part 34 as a baseline and holding its solar assumptions constant, ARK has modeled a scenario in which nuclear provides all power except that from solar. Please see the resulting capacity requirements below.

Source: ARK Investment Management LLC, 2025, based on data from Lovering et al. 2016; IAEA 2023; EIA 2022 ; World Nuclear News 2022; Our World in Data 2023; and Tesla 2023 as of October 2023.5 For informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security. Past performance is not indicative of future results. Forecasts are inherently limited and cannot be relied upon.

Our calculations suggest that while solar costs are likely to continue their decline, nuclear costs have the potential to decline at a more rapid rate. Here we propose four possible nuclear cost scenarios, their data incorporated in the chart below:

1. Step-Change Cost Decline To Pre-1975 Nuclear Cost Trendline:

  • A discontinuous drop in costs is possible as new companies like Oklo (OKLO) execute their plans, pushing costs down to their pre-1975 trendline. This cost decline, shown by the red dotted line below, would result in overall capital costs of approximately $3.3 trillion, or $1 trillion on a capacity-adjusted basis.

2. Gradual Cost Decline To Pre-1975 Nuclear Cost Trendline:

  • Costs fall gradually to the trend in place before 1975. This cost decline, shown in the navy dotted line below, would result in overall capital costs of approximately $6.4 trillion, or $1.8 trillion on a capacity adjusted basis.

3. Initial Cost Overruns, Followed By A Decline To Pre-1975 Nuclear Cost Trendline:

  • In line with a Tennessee Valley Authority study,6 the first small modular nuclear reactors are more expensive, but costs decline over time to the trend in place before 1975. This cost decline, shown in the orange dotted line below, would result in overall capital costs of approximately $9.3 trillion, or $2.7 trillion on a capacity adjusted basis.

4. Inability To Hit The Pre-1975 Cost-Decline Trendline:

  • From today’s levels, costs fall in line with Wright’s Law7 and never drop to the trend in place before 1975. This cost decline, as shown in the pink dotted line below, would result in overall capital costs of approximately $26.5 trillion, or $7.6 trillion on a capacity adjusted basis.

Source: ARK Investment Management LLC, 2025, based on data from Lovering et al. 2016; IAEA 2023; EIA 2022 ; World Nuclear News 2022; Our World in Data 2023; and Tesla 2023 as of October 2023.8 For informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security. Past performance is not indicative of future results. Forecasts are inherently limited and cannot be relied upon.

In our view, solar should continue to grow, its costs either declining at a consistent rate, as shown in the dotted green line below, or reverting to their historic cost decline rate, as shown in the dotted purple line below. Based on the slope of the two cost declines, the capital cost to move from the current state to the future end state would be approximately $2.9 trillion to $3.4 trillion, respectively.

According to Tesla, ultimately solar would require 71.4 million acres of land which, according to our research, would add another $700 billion to overall costs.

Source: ARK Investment Management LLC, 2025, based on data from Our World in Data 2023 as of October 2023.9 For informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security. Past performance is not indicative of future results.

The future of nuclear energy depends upon costs. If a company were to achieve a discontinuous decline in costs, nuclear power could emerge as the lowest-cost source of electricity. If not, regulatory changes from the 1970s probably have limited its long-term viability.

That said, solar’s land requirement at 71.4 million acres would be a considerable logistical challenge. Because solar development involves complex real estate negotiations and long interconnect times, scaling would be difficult, leaving a place for alternative power sources like nuclear.

Footnotes

  • 1 Korus, S. 2025. “Regulations Have Prevented Electricity Price Declines In The US.” ARK Investment Management LLC.
  • 2 Lovering, J. et al. 2016. “Historical construction costs of global nuclear power reactors.” Energy Policy. International Energy Agency. 2022. “Cost and Performance Characteristics of New Generating Technologies, Annual Energy Outlook 2022.” World Nuclear News. 2022. “AP1000 remains attractive option for US market, says MIT.” Our World in Data. 2023. “Solar (photovoltaic) panel prices vs. cumulative capacity.”
  • 3 Lovering, J. et al. 2016. “Historical construction costs of global nuclear power reactors.” Energy Policy. International Energy Agency. 2022. “Cost and Performance Characteristics of New Generating Technologies, Annual Energy Outlook 2022.” World Nuclear News. 2022. “AP1000 remains attractive option for US market, says MIT.” Our World in Data. 2023. “Solar (photovoltaic) panel prices vs. cumulative capacity.”
  • 4 Tesla. 2023. “Master Plan Part 3 Sustainable Energy for All of Earth.”
  • 5 Lovering, J. et al. 2016. “Historical construction costs of global nuclear power reactors.” Energy Policy. International Energy Agency. 2022. “Cost and Performance Characteristics of New Generating Technologies, Annual Energy Outlook 2022.” World Nuclear News. 2022. “AP1000 remains attractive option for US market, says MIT.” Tesla. 2023. “Master Plan Part 3 Sustainable Energy for All of Earth.”
  • 6 Tennessee Valley Authority. 2024. “Integrated Resource Plan 2025.”
  • 7 Wright’s Law states that for every cumulative doubling of units produced, costs will fall by a constant percentage. See Winton, B. 2019. “Moore’s Law Isn’t Dead: It’s Wrong—Long Live Wright’s Law.” ARK Investment Management LLC.
  • 8 Lovering, J. et al. 2016. “Historical construction costs of global nuclear power reactors.” Energy Policy. International Energy Agency. 2022. “Cost and Performance Characteristics of New Generating Technologies, Annual Energy Outlook 2022.” World Nuclear News. 2022. “AP1000 remains attractive option for US market, says MIT.” Our World in Data. 2023. “Solar (photovoltaic) panel prices vs. cumulative capacity.” Tesla. 2023. “Master Plan Part 3 Sustainable Energy for All of Earth.”
  • 9 Our World in Data. 2023. “Solar (photovoltaic) panel prices vs. cumulative capacity.”

Disclosure

ARK's statements are not an endorsement of any company or a recommendation to buy, sell or hold any security. For a list of all purchases and sales made by ARK for client accounts during the past year that could be considered by the SEC as recommendations, click here. It should not be assumed that recommendations made in the future will be profitable or will equal the performance of the securities in this list. For full disclosures, click here.

data.title: The Switch: Challenges In Forecasting GDP Growth For Q1 And Q2 2025
data.description:
By Pat Higgins

The US Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA) estimated that real GDP contracted at an annualized rate of 0.3 percent in the first quarter of 2025, the first negative reading since the first quarter of 2022. As figure 1 shows, this reading was still well above the final nowcast of -2.7 percent growth for what was then the standard GDPNow model and meaningfully above, but closer to, the final "gold-adjusted" model forecast of -1.5 percent.

Figure 1: Evolution of Atlanta Fed GDPNow real estimate for 2025:Q1

As detailed here, the gold-adjusted model—unlike the former version—subtracts international trade in gold when estimating net exports of goods. Otherwise, the models are essentially identical, and both of their final projections have the change in private inventories (CIPI) adding 0.30 percentage points to first-quarter real GDP growth.

The April 29 GDPNow nowcasts were well below the BEA's estimate that CIPI added 2.25 percentage points, accounting for the largest subcomponent contribution forecast error for either of the models. The excerpt below from the GDP release gives a clue as to why the models were so wrong on this CIPI subcomponent (boldface added):

The largest contributor to the increase in investment was private inventory investment, led by an increase in wholesale trade (notably, drugs and sundries). The estimates of private inventory investment were based primarily on Census Bureau inventory book value data and a BEA adjustment in March to account for a notable increase in imports. For more information on the source data and BEA assumptions for inventories, refer to the key source data and assumptions table (available at 10 a.m.).

It's unclear from the release what the size of BEA's March adjustment was. However, if we take the March values for merchant and nonmerchant wholesale inventories (provided in the BEA's key source data and assumptions and investment related underlying detail files) and simply plug them in place of the GDPNow forecasts for those values in the Inventories tab of the April 29 GDPNow spreadsheet, the model's CIPI contribution forecast goes up by 0.84 percentage points. Using the model's monthly inventories data-based forecast rather than averaging it with the quarterly-based model forecast, as described on page 17 here, further increases the CIPI contribution to 1.02 percentage points. The estimate cited in the Wall Street Journal assesses the impact of the adjustment to the March inventories data on GDP growth to be 1.2 percentage points.

Real GDP growth was slightly negative despite the large CIPI contribution and a solid 3.0 percent real growth rate for aggregate personal consumption expenditures (PCE) and private fixed investment primarily because net exports subtracted 4.83 percentage points off first-quarter real GDP growth, as shown in the third bar in figure 2. BEA data show that nearly all of the reduction (4.79 percentage points) was accounted for by a 50.9 percent annualized surge in real goods imports that, apart from the pandemic related rebound in the third quarter of 2020, was the largest increase since 1972. In theory, this gain should be offset in the CIPI and/or other spending subcomponents of GDP. But the aforementioned BEA inventories adjustment shows that this cancellation may not be evident in the published monthly GDP source data.

It's unclear whether the reverse phenomenon—spending on goods drawn from inventories that are not accounted for in the published Census Bureau inventories data—can or will occur. But we can anticipate that it is likely that either the BEA's estimate of inventories contribution to first-quarter GDP growth will be revised down or GDPNow's projected contribution of it to second-quarter GDP growth will be revised down on June 27. Until June 27, GDPNow will make its own calculation of first-quarter CIPI in GDP using Census Bureau data on the book-value of inventories and BEA data for the remainder of the CIPI related data. This is because using Census Bureau book-value data usually generates virtually the same CIPI estimate for the prior quarter as what one would get using only the BEA data immediately after the GDP release. This allowed the model to anticipate BEA revisions of CIPI for the prior quarter in second and third release GDP estimates after Census Bureau revisions to monthly inventory book values. However, GDPNow currently calculates a first-quarter annualized CIPI of $94 billion in 2017 dollars, while the BEA calculated it as $140 billion. The published first-quarter CIPI will be revised two more times by the BEA on May 29 and June 26. After the latter revision, the BEA's first-quarter CIPI will be "final" until the annual revision that will take place on September 26 if the BEA follows the same annual revision scheduling pattern as it did in 2022–24. With respect to the July 30, 2025:Q2 GDP release, 2025:Q1 CIPI will be "frozen" at the level published in the June 26 GDP (third) release estimate. So GDPNow would switch to the temporarily "frozen" BEA estimate on June 27 (see also the third-to-last FAQ here and/or more formal discussion on pages 13–17 here). If both GDPNow and the BEA estimates for CIPI remain at their current, but different, estimates through June 26, the GDPNow switch to the higher 2025:Q1 value for CIPI would reduce its topline nowcast by 0.8 percentage points on June 27.

Figure 1 also shows that when we extend the gold-adjusted model forecasts from when it went "live" on March 6 back to February 26—the last forecast date before January international trade data with the surge in gold imports were released—the standard and gold-adjusted model forecasts are nearly identical in this final pre–gold import surge forecast. In the historical forecast accuracy analysis below, we use what was then the GDPNow growth forecasts for the first quarter of 2025 prior to February 26 and the gold-adjusted growth forecasts for the first quarter of 2025 beginning on this date.

Figure 2: Actual Historical and Forecasted 2025:Q2 Contributions to Real GDP Growth

percentage points, SAAR

Figure 2 shows Blue Chip and GDPNow forecasts for the composition of second quarter real GDP growth. The Blue Chip forecast includes all subcomponents shown in the chart except for "government plus residential investment." We essentially derive this as a residual using the topline GDP and other subcomponent forecasts along with some chain-weighting calculations. The current and now defunct GDPNow forecasts are also shown in the figure, the latter of which doesn't include the gold adjustments to the international trade data described here. The latter being stronger than the former implies that the model sees (net) gold imports falling this quarter relative to the first, which is plausible given the sharp decline in gold imports from January ($32.6 billion) to March ($17.5 billion).

Figure 2 also shows the consensus Blue Chip Economic Indicators (BCEI) forecast for the second quarter of 2025 is weaker than the most recent GDPNow forecast as well as a typical quarter, proxied by the left-most two bars showing average annualized growth between the fourth quarter of 2007 and the fourth quarter of 2019 National Bureau of Economic Research business cycle peaks, and average growth since the latter peak. The subcomponent composition of the Blue Chip consensus forecast is also quite different than any of the others shown. It has both net exports and inventories partially reversing their first-quarter contributions but netting out to a positive, rather than a negative, total contribution. Its remaining subcomponent contributions are negative on balance and all somewhat weaker than their corresponding, "typical," first-quarter, and GDPNow-projected values. GDPNow was about as weak on May 1 as the Blue Chip forecast after it first incorporated April "soft data" in the ISM Manufacturing and consumer attitudes reports. But it was revised up the next day after incorporating better April data on employment and motor vehicle sales.

How might we assess the uncertainty around the Blue Chip and GDPNow forecasts? Prior to the pandemic, GDPNow projections two-and-a-half-months ahead were generally moderately less accurate than professional forecasts, but they were about on par with them within a month of the release. And since 2020, GDPNow has been less accurate on average than it was prior to the pandemic. In particular, using the gold-adjusted model for the first quarter of 2025, from the first quarter of 2021 to the first quarter of 2025, GDPNow's average absolute error for its final forecasts (0.65 percentage points, using a seasonally adjusted annual rate) was greater than it was in its pre-pandemic history (from the second quarter of 2014 to the fourth quarter of 2019) since it was first published (0.51 percentage points). Moreover, 80-day ahead pre-pandemic forecasts over this span were about as accurate, on average, as 20-day ahead forecasts starting in the first quarter of 2021.

The pandemic posed a number of forecasting challenges, including fiscal stimulus and supply chain disruptions, that made forecasting more difficult and probably less accurate than it was in the 2010s. The difficulty of forecasting also increases as the probability of a recession rises. The subdued Blue Chip forecast of GDP growth in the second quarter of 2025 is very similar to the consensus in the April Wall Street Journal Economic Forecasting Survey, and the respondents in that WSJ survey put the 12-month recession probability at 45 percent on average. Although lower than the 48 percent to 63 percent recession probability range prevailing in the survey during the second half of 2022 and all of 2023, this latest reading is well above its 17 percent average seen during 2014–19. All of this suggests that, in a probabilistic sense, we should expect forecasts in the second quarter of 2025 to be less accurate than they were in 2014–2019. For some additional (and more formal) statistical evidence, as seen in the chart in GDPNow's home page and here, the difference—or so-called "forecast disagreement"—between the average of the 10 highest Blue Chip forecasts and the average of the 10 lowest Blue Chip forecasts is much larger in the most recent BCEI survey than it was in late March. Research by Constantin Bürgi and Tara Sinclair (you can also see it here) shows that increased forecaster disagreement in two-and-a-half-month ahead GDP growth expectations are significantly associated with higher recession odds in a probit model. Further, a regression analysis I did with the historical BCEI data (excluding the first three quarters of 2020) implies that the current "Top 10-Bottom 10" BCEI forecast disagreement is associated with an 80-day ahead GDP growth nowcast that is 0.92 percentage points less accurate than it would be if that disagreement was instead at its 2014–19 average. With these differences in mind, figure 3 shows the average absolute forecast errors for the 80-day ahead GDPNow and BCEI real GDP growth forecasts and its associated subcomponent contributions beginning in the first quarter of 2021.

Figure 3: GDP Subcomponent Average Absolute Forecast Errors for 80-Day Ahead GDPNow and Blue Chip Economic Indicators Projections of Contributions to Growth Since 2021:Q1

The Blue Chip forecasts for topline growth are a bit less accurate, on average, than GDPNow. On the other hand, they are a bit more accurate for their subcomponent contribution forecasts. Evidently, GDPNow had a more fortuitous cancellation of subcomponent errors than the BCEI did. Nevertheless, these accuracy metrics are of similar magnitude. Since the average absolute forecast error is equal to (√(2/π)) ≈ 0.8 standard deviations in a Gaussian distribution, the post-2020 period accuracy standard suggests that a negative GDP growth rate in the second quarter of 2025 is within the 70 percent confidence interval. But a fairly solid-to-strong growth rate is also within it. Figure 3 also shows that real PCE, CIPI, and net exports account for the largest, and similarly sized, forecast errors.

Figure 4 is comparable to figure 3 but uses shorter-term forecasts. The 20-day ahead Blue Chip forecast errors of real GDP growth are larger than those for GDPNow, but—as we noted before—the subcomponent contribution forecast errors are comparable. I should note that some luck is also evident when we compare GDPNow's 20-day ahead nowcast with its final forecasts, as the latter topline nowcasts are slightly less accurate than the former despite the improved accuracy of the final nowcasts for real PCE, CIPI, and net exports. For these shortest-horizon forecasts, the PCE contribution error metric is smaller than the same metrics for CIPI and net exports. Much of the final PCE forecast improvement relative to its 20-day ahead forecast is the result of incorporating a final retail sales release for the quarter 10 to 12 days before the GDP release.

Figure 4: GDP Subcomponent Average Absolute Forecast Errors for 20-Day or Less Ahead GDPNow and Blue Chip Economic Indicators Projections of Contributions to Growth

It's an open question whether another judgmental BEA adjustment to CIPI, or perhaps one of the other subcomponents, could again factor into the forecast errors for GDP growth in the second quarter of 2025. But the above analysis and figures suggest a reasonable likelihood of being able to rule out either negative or relatively strong GDP growth this quarter as we approach the release date. The analysis also shows that net exports and CIPI are among the more difficult subcomponents to forecast—and that difficulty is present even without the added uncertainty surrounding tariffs

Original Post

data.title: Bursa Malaysia: Divergent Prospects For Its Major Businesses
data.description:
I'm still Neutral on Bursa Malaysia Berhad (OTCPK:BSMAF) (BMYS.KL) or "BMB".

BMB's pros and cons as an investment candidate were detailed in the previous August 30, 2024 update.

My Hold rating for BMB is unchanged. The company's ST ("Securities or Equities Trading") division is under pressure in the short term. But I anticipate that both its DT ("Derivatives Trading") and NTR ("Non Trading Revenue") units will deliver strong results going forward.

Weak Equity Sentiment Will Weigh On Securities Business

ST is BMB's biggest segment, representing half of its FY24 sales.

Malaysia's ADTV ("Average Daily Trading Value") for equities fell by 10% year-on-year ("YoY") to RM$2.6B in 1Q25. During the same time frame, BMB saw its ST topline decrease by -12%.

The underperformance of BMB's top revenue-generating unit drove a -9% drop in its first quarter net profit to MYR 68M. This was 7% lower than the analysts' consensus forecast, according to S&P Capital IQ.

The market was already worried about trade tensions in early-2025 when the Trump administration took office. Non-domestic investors divested over US$2B worth of Malaysia-listed shares in the initial three months of this year.

Things have gotten worse with the announcement of "Liberation Day tariffs".

Last month, BMB's securities ADTV went down further to MYR 2,200 million ("source: S&P Capital IQ"). It is equivalent to a 15 percent sequential decline between 1Q2025 and Apr-25. This operating metric was also +50% higher at RM$3.3 billion for full-year 2024.

We have seen the Sino-American "trade war truce" and a "tariff pause" for other nations happen. But the above-mentioned are agreements lasting for no more than a quarter.

A sustained recovery in Malaysian stock market activity is unlikely unless the U.S. and its trading partners sign permanent deals to resolve their differences. My sensitivity analysis indicates that BMB's bottom line could decrease by 5-6% for every -10 percent equity ADTV contraction.

Volume Of Hedging And Listing Activities Should Stay High In The Near Future

BMB generated 32% and 15% of its top line from NTR and DT, respectively, in the prior fiscal year. These two divisions have been bright spots for the company.

There is another remaining 3% of BMB's revenue derived from other income streams, which I won't discuss here since they are immaterial.

In 1Q25, the company's DT segment sales rose by +14% YoY to RM$29 million.

The ADV ("Average Daily Volumes") for BMB's CPO ("Crude Palm Oil") and KLCI ("Malaysian stock benchmark Kuala Lumpur Composite Index") futures grew by 25% and 4%, respectively for the first quarter.

It's reasonable to assume that commodity prices and stock indices will most probably remain volatile with the ongoing tariff war. The derivative instruments offered by BMB should be in demand due to hedging needs.

Separately, its NTR operations also registered a positive topline expansion of +6% year-on-year for 1Q2025.

In a difficult market environment like this, some Malaysian companies will engage in accelerated repurchases to support their stock prices. At the same time, certain bearish investors will borrow shares as part of short-selling transactions. These actions translated into an increase in non-trading fees for BMB.

For the full year, I think IPOs ("listing revenue") will be a major growth engine for BMB's NTR unit.

There were 14 public listings in Malaysia for 2025 YTD ("Year-To-Date"), which is better than the nine for the same period last year.

BMB's goal is to have 60 new entities list on the Malaysian exchange in FY25, or five more than the 2024 IPO volume.

My take is that this target is feasible. Equity market valuations might trend down and reduce the potential IPO proceeds. But many unlisted corporates might want to strengthen their balance sheets with fresh equity financing ("via IPOs"). The intention could be to guard against a potential funding drought in a recession.

I expect that the strength of BMB's DT and NTR businesses will help to partially offset the drag from the core ST segment.

My Final Thoughts

I don't have good reasons to be strongly bullish or bearish on the stock. BMB's risk-reward is fair, taking into account its outlook and valuations.

I'm forecasting a low-teens EPS decline for BMB in FY2025. The group's net income should subsequently rebound by a mid-single digit percentage for both FY26/27 based on my projections. This assumes that trade tensions continue for the rest of this year before easing in the future.

My forecast implies that BMB's FY24-27 bottom line CAGR will be close to zero. Bursa's Hold rating is justified by flattish medium-term growth expectations.

BMB also appears to be reasonably valued based on a peer comparison.

BMB is now trading at 22.4x ("source: S&P Capital IQ") NTM ("Next Twelve Months") P/E. Another Southeast Asian exchange operator, Singapore Exchange Limited (OTCPK:SPXCY) (OTCPK:SPXCF), boasts a forward earnings multiple of 23.6 times. That's pretty close.
